statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, implement a function to push and pop elements from a stack in any order. The function should return true if and only if the elements in the array are popped out in the same order they are pushed.
statement.inputFormat: Array of integers representing elements to be pushed onto the stack
statement.outputFormat: True if elements are popped in same order as pushed, False otherwise
statement.notes: Use a stack data structure to solve this problem

examples.input: push and pop from an empty stack
examples.explanation: Push and pop from an empty stack

examples.input: push and pop from a stack with 1 element
examples.explanation: Push and pop from a stack with 1 element

hints.text: The problem can be solved using a stack data structure.

editorial.title: Infinite Loop in Stack Implementation
editorial.intuition: The main idea is to push all elements onto the stack, then pop elements from the stack and compare with the original array. If elements are popped in the same order as pushed, return true; otherwise, return false.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force solution involves pushing and popping elements from the stack in a loop, which can lead to an infinite loop if not properly checked.
editorial.optimalSolution: Optimal solution involves using a stack data structure and iterating over the given array to push and pop elements in a loop.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The solution is correct because it checks whether elements are popped in the same order as pushed, which is the core requirement of the problem.
editorial.dryRun: Example run: push all elements onto the stack, then pop elements from the stack and compare with the original array.
commonMistakes: Infinite loop due to improper checking

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool validateStackSequences(vector<int>& pushed, vector<int>& popped); };
starterCode.java: public class Solution { public boolean validateStackSequences(int[] pushed, int[] popped); }
starterCode.python: class Solution: def validateStackSequences(self, pushed: List[int], popped: List[int]) -> bool: pass
starterCode.javascript: function validateStackSequences(pushed, popped) { /* your code here */ }

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: bool validateStackSequences(vector<int>& pushed, vector<int>& popped) { stack<int> s; int p = 0; for (int x : pushed) s.push(x); while (p < popped.size()) { if (popped[p] == s.top()) s.pop(); else s.push(pushed[p]); p++; } return true; } };
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public boolean validateStackSequences(int[] pushed, int[] popped) { Stack<Integer> s = new Stack<>(); int p = 0; for (int x : pushed) s.push(x); while (p < popped.length) { if (popped[p] == s.peek()) s.pop(); else s.push(pushed[p]); p++; } return true; } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def validateStackSequences(self, pushed: List[int], popped: List[int]) -> bool: s = [] p = 0; for x in pushed: s.append(x); while p < len(popped): if popped[p] == s[-1]: s.pop(); else s.append(pushed[p]); p++; return True;
buggyStarterCode.javascript: function validateStackSequences(pushed, popped) { var s = []; var p = 0; for (var x of pushed) s.push(x); while (p < popped.length) { if (popped[p] == s[s.length - 1]) s.pop(); else s.push(pushed[p]); p++; } return true; }
